On 10 March, the world's first woman cosmonaut, United Russia State Duma deputy Valentina Tereshkova, unexpectedly proposed "zeroing out" Vladimir Putin's presidential terms and thus allowing him to run again (the Duma and the Federation Council had already approved Tereshkova's amendment). It was suggested that the deputy, who rarely speaks in the Duma, made her proposal at the Kremlin's request. Tereshkova, during her Duma speech, claimed she did it at the request of voters. On 11 March she repeated this version in an interview with Komsomolskaya Pravda. According to Tereshkova, she proposed giving Putin the chance to run for president again because ordinary people, not political circles, had asked her to.

Semyon Skrepetsky, oil on board (pine). Painting. Dimensions 28/34 cm.

Moscow. 9 March.
— US President Donald Trump welcomed the fall in world oil prices.

"Good for the consumer, gasoline prices coming down," he wrote on Twitter on Monday.

Oil fell more than 25% on Monday after the collapse of the OPEC+ deal and Saudi Arabia's decision to increase output and cut prices, despite falling demand for hydrocarbons on the world market. Brent crude futures fell 26.5%, reaching $33.3 a barrel at 9:30 Moscow time. American WTI traded around $29.8 a barrel, down 27.5%.

"The Ziggurat of Worse-Than-Calamity." Oil painting, Semyon Skrepetsky, painting. Oil on board, 44/31 cm.

In June 2020 a temple of war was built in Russia (the Ziggurat of Worse-Than-Calamity).

It was built with both donations and budget funds from Moscow and the Moscow region. The consecration took place on 14 June 2020 as part of the celebrations of the 75th anniversary of the Victory in the Great Patriotic War. The temple immediately became a repository of strange "artifacts."

"There are unique items there, right down to Hitler's suit, which has survived to this day. There is Hitler's cap."
(c) Timur Ivanov.

Russian Deputy Defense Minister Timur Ivanov spoke about unique items belonging to the founder of the Third Reich, Adolf Hitler. They are kept at the ministry's memorial complex "Road of Memory."

According to Ivanov, the complex has special air-purification systems installed so as not to damage the costly exhibits, including the icons of the Cathedral in honor of the Resurrection of Christ (the main temple of the Russian Armed Forces). "The same installations are located right in the 'Road of Memory,' because there are genuine relics there. There are unique items, right down to Hitler's suit, which has survived to this day. There is Hitler's cap," said the Russian Deputy Defense Minister in an interview with the Zvezda TV channel.

"Initiation Into Governors." Semyon Skrepetsky.
Oil painting. 28/20 cm. Oil on board, painting.
Once, through a Kremlin oversight, a Putinist blunder, Moscow allowed Khabarovsk to elect its own governor, Sergei Furgal. And this governor began doing incomprehensible things — working for the people. Now free lunches in school cafeterias, now repaired hospitals. Sheer Russophobia and discrediting of Vladimir Putin. And the foolish Khabarovsk people began to respect this governor of theirs, and one day a poll showed that the people trusted their governor more than Volodya the Sun-Faced. Volodya learned of such vile treachery and sent from the capital city to Khabarovsk his zealous oprichniki, loyal to the tsar. Three days and three nights the oprichniki galloped, galloped and galloped, galloped and galloped, until they broke their rectal bottles. At last they galloped into Khabarovsk, caught the negligent governor, beat him brutally and threw him in prison. They invented a criminal case for Furgal and sent him to penal servitude.
And in place of Furgal a new governor was brought to Khabarovsk, young and vetted, from the harem of the Moscow deputy Volodya Zhirinovsky. The name of that aged harem-boy was Mishka Degtyarev. He was a droll rascal, a lad good for anything (he both took it in the mouth and received it under the tail), and Zhirinovsky loved him for it, for he himself had repeatedly taken Mishka to the sauna and extracted an oath of loyalty from him.

And so the people of Khabarovsk lived with the new governor, lived without trouble and prospered, and whoever opened his mouth out of turn was sat on a bottle. And Furgal was forgotten.

This canvas captures the moment of initiation into governorship. The future governor drops the soap during the ritual, but the caring leader of the LPDR party helps him pick it up.

This painting captures the moment when Alexander Lukashenko does not cling to his chair with blue fingers.

The 2020 elections.
On 9 August 2020 the regular elections were held. On 14 August the official results were announced: 80.1% of voters, according to the Central Election Commission, were cast for Lukashenko.

The elections were rigged. The dissenting people of Belarus poured into the streets en masse, but the rallies were brutally suppressed by the riot police. Stun grenades and rubber bullets were used against the protesters.

November 2019.
Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko told journalists at a polling station in Minsk that he would take part in the upcoming head-of-state elections. He promised not to take offense at citizens if they did not support him.

"If I feel that you are categorically against me, of course I'll be upset, but nevertheless you must reject my candidacy at the elections. And I promised that I will not cling to the chair with blue fingers. Believe me, it's not exactly a soft chair."

The phenomenon of the Russian world has always been that Russia was never self-sufficient. In Soviet times it bought or stole equipment and technology; in post-Soviet times it sold raw materials and kept the money in more developed countries, buying real estate there.
Russian patriots always drove foreign cars and dressed in foreign brands. The entire Russian elite owned real estate in developed European countries.

For more than two decades, Putin very deftly traded in the ashes of his ancestors. Every year the Russian president served up the Soviet Union's victory over Hitler's Germany to the Russian people as his own personal achievement. The Russian people took pride in the leader.
— Glory to the great leader! — shouted the Russian Vanya, having gulped down a glass of industrial alcohol and kissed a portrait of Putin.

The Russian leader understood that the Russian loves to take pride in the imaginary feats of his ancestors, and so very often Russian propaganda told of the unprecedented exploits of forefathers, explaining that Putin is an evident admirer of ancient traditions and, if need be, could "do it again, like the granddads."
